pageTitle.value: Perpetual Planet - An expedition to the Yucatán cave systems

pageCaption.value: Pollution from cities and tourism is contaminating the aquifer that is Yucatán’s sole source of fresh water. Six of the world’s best cave divers spent two weeks on a Perpetual Planet-supported expedition mapping cave systems and taking water samples from sinkholes that were explored for the first time in the modern era.

WaPageTitle.value: Yucatán, Mexico — A giant aquifer under siege

metadescription.value: Robbie Schmittner led a scientific expedition to the caves of Mexico's Yucatán Peninsula to find a solution to water pollution. More on Rolex.org.

text.value: Pollution from cities and tourism is contaminating the aquifer that is the sole source of fresh water in the Yucatán Peninsula. Six of the world’s best divers spent two weeks on a Perpetual Planet-supported expedition mapping cave systems and taking water samples from sinkholes that were explored for the first time in the modern era.

title.value: Armed with a light and a line

text.value: First he came as a tourist, then he stayed as a cave-diving guide. Now he dives for science. For more than 20 years, since he arrived from Germany, underwater cave explorer Robbie Schmittner has slipped into the sinkholes scattered in their thousands across Mexico’s Yucatán Peninsula, to explore a vast, subterranean world, armed with a light and thin nylon line that prevent him from getting lost. It also doubles as an efficient way, even in the 21st century, to collect measurements to help chart the caves.

In November 2021, Schmittner led a team of professional divers on the Xunaan-Ha Expedition, a two-week undertaking to explore five sinkholes and their underlying caves suspected of connecting the Holbox Fracture, literally a series of cracks in the limestone plateau thought to extend from Belize to just past Cancún in Mexico.

As a result of the mission – supported by Rolex as part of its Perpetual Planet initiative – Schmittner is a step closer to shedding new light on rainwater drainage across the Yucatán, with the twin discovery of cave systems that do indeed link to the Holbox Fracture and a new fault he has named the Akumal Fracture. He and his team, all of them women – Tamara Lelani May (Australia), Ellen Cuylaerts (Belgium), Maria Bollerup (Denmark), Julia Gugelmeier (Germany), Rannva Joermundsson (Faroe Islands) and Melodie Trevino (Mexico) – were also able to take water samples from nine sites near Tulum that will form a baseline for future water-quality testing.

Schmittner’s ultimate ambition is to provide the scientific basis for tackling human pollution of the giant aquifer, which stretches 165,000 km² across the Yucatán Peninsula, all the way to Guatemala and Belize. The aquifer, one of the biggest in the world, is the region’s only source of fresh water.

“A better understanding of the natural drainage system is essential for protecting ecosystems – the savannah, the rainforest – that depend on the aquifer for water,” he says. “Independent scientific projects, such as for the protection of the jaguar and mangrove forests, the preservation of the nature reserve areas or the replanting of coral reefs, would be much more efficient if the course of the groundwater were known, as in some cases it also comes with deadly pollution. We need to find the sources of contamination.”

“Knowledge about the cave systems is hidden in many places. So far, about only 1,600 to 2,000 km have been explored,” says Schmittner, who has mapped some 400 km of the caves himself, in the process discovering the longest water-filled cave network in the world by connecting the Sac Actun and Dos Ojos systems. On this latest expedition, the team charted some 3,214 m of difficult cave passages, a “strikingly impressive” distance in such a short time, he says.

“It’s like being on another planet. It is beautiful. You just descend through a sinkhole and at 25°C, the water is warm and crystal clear. In the dark, all you have is your capsule of light and you just fly along.” The caves are winding, some gigantic, some small, some like tubes worn smooth over millions of years, or with stalagmites and stalactites. There is the sensation of being the first person to have explored the caves, but relics from the last ice age prove this cannot be true. During that period, water levels were not so high and people were frequently in the caves for water.

Inevitably, cave exploring carries its own set of risks. Besides the possibility of losing the guide line, Schmittner often gets stuck in small caves. “It is stressful,” he says, “but you got in so you can get out, even if the cave does not want to let you go and you have a stalagmite stuck in your jacket.

The Yucatán Peninsula is remarkable for its landscape. There are no rivers or lakes and little soil. When it rains, the porous limestone acts as a sponge. Rainfall trickles down until it meets salt water that has similarly seeped in from the ocean. Fresh water is not as dense, allowing it to sit on top of the salt water in the aquifer and be pumped up for consumption.

However, the fresh water continues to find ways to escape through fractures in the limestone, some of which were made by the strike of the Chicxulub meteorite some 66 million years ago. The fractures redirect the water and the result is an upside-down world, with a delta that is assumed to have three interconnecting underground rivers, with a cave roof instead of the sky.

Using information from satellite images, cave exploration and an understanding of inland and ocean currents, Schmittner has formulated a big picture of the drainage system. “Viewed as a fact that the principal fractures are related, it is possible that most of the precipitation collects and is directed to the Caribbean coast in the fractures and their cave systems,” he says. “Massive suction is caused by powerful currents from South America that are dramatically constricted as they pass the Yucatán Canal on their way to the Gulf of Mexico. This draws fresh water from the Holbox and Akumal fractures, which run close to the Caribbean coast where the limestone is comparatively thin, and draws it up to the north-eastern tip of the peninsula. This is how 2,000 km of cave passages were created along the Caribbean coast.”

The facts are stark in the Yucatán. The population of some 2.3 million is swelled by 6 million tourists annually, with pressure to attract more. Tourism is the only real industry in the region as the soil is too poor for agriculture. But development on the peninsula has nevertheless taken place, using fresh water from the aquifer without much regard for its fragility and vulnerability, according to Schmittner.

Most of the effluent finds its way into the aquifer through overflowing septic tanks and open dumps. “There is no sewage treatment in Tulum and the situation is similar in other places,” he adds. “The water comes from the south-west in a relatively clean state, becomes contaminated and goes out to the ocean via the Akumal and Holbox fractures, polluting ecosystems on the way.”

As a result of the expedition, the geology, hydrology and water chemistry of the cave systems will be analysed by university departments in Mexico and the United States. In the near future, Schmittner plans to set up a Water and Jungle Institute in Tulum to provide education, research, consulting and advice. “Masters and PhD students will conduct research there, with implications globally as aquifers in coastal and arid regions are under threat. We need to preserve them for generations to come.”

pageCaption.value: The history of Rolex and exploration is one linked to some of the greatest adventures of the past century. Oyster watches have been to the top of the world and the deepest part of the ocean. For generations, pioneering explorers have attested to their reliability in the toughest of conditions.

metadescription.value: Discover Rolex's inspiring history of robust innovation, closely linked to the greatest mountaineering, polar and diving expeditions of the past century.

pageTitle.value: Mt Everest sensors give new insights into climate change

pageCaption.value: In May 2019, as part of its Perpetual Planet partnership with Rolex, National Geographic succeeded in leading an expedition to install the world’s highest weather station on Mt Everest.

WaPageTitle.value: Mt Everest sensors give new insights into climate change

metadescription.value: Discover how, as part of Perpetual Planet, Rolex and National Geographic mounted a successful expedition to install the world’s highest weather station.

pageTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Adventures in the underworld

pageCaption.value: The hidden realm of caves has opened up a new world to scientists and explorers thanks to speleologists such as Francesco Sauro whose many expeditions have provided an archive of time for future generations.

WaPageTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Venturer in the underworld

metadescription.value: Rolex Awards Laureate and speleologist Francesco Sauro explores the world's deepest caves to uncover previously unknown life forms. More on rolex.org.
